title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita

Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

The chart displays five national time series for real gross domestic product per capita from the year 1000 to 2018, measured in 2011 US dollars adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity. For the first eight centuries shown, all five lines — representing Japan, the United Kingdom, Italy, China, and India — remain clustered near the bottom of the vertical axis, mostly below $5,000. Italy’s green line fluctuates slightly above the others between 1300 and 1600, hovering around $2,000 to $3,000, while the other four stay closer to $1,000 or less. Beginning around 1800, the United Kingdom’s blue line and Japan’s orange line begin a sustained upward climb, crossing $5,000 by 1850 and accelerating sharply after 1900. By 2018, both reach values between $35,000 and $40,000. Italy’s line follows a similar trajectory but remains slightly lower, ending near $35,000. China’s purple line and India’s black line remain flat near the baseline until approximately 1980, after which both rise steeply; China reaches about $15,000 by 2018, while India ends just under $10,000. The visual structure shows a long period of minimal change followed by rapid divergence among countries in the last two centuries, with the steepest growth occurring after 1950 for all except China and India, whose sharp increases are concentrated in the final four decades. All values are expressed in constant 2011 US dollars to enable cross-country and cross-time comparison of economic output per person.
